param_input_item.param_description: a character vector, string array, or cell array of character vectors.

param_output_item.param_description: match indices, matches, tokens, named token structures, or split text.

description.p: regexp searches text using Perl-compatible regular expressions.

A regular expression describes a pattern rather than a literal string. Use it when the text can vary, for example when matching identifiers, dates, email addresses, numbers, tags, or alternative spellings.

A typical workflow is to identify the pieces of text that are unique, translate each piece into regular expression syntax, and then call regexp with the output key that returns the information you need.
By default, regexp returns 1-based start indices for every non-overlapping match. Matching continues after the end of the previous match. Use the 'once' option to stop after the first match.

tr.td: one character not in a set
'\w+\s+\w+' matches two words separated by spaces

description.p: Quantifiers specify how many times the preceding expression can occur.

tr.td: at least m and at most n times

description.p: Quantifiers are greedy by default. Add ? after a quantifier for a lazy match, such as '.*?' . Add + after a quantifier for a possessive match, such as '.*+' .
Grouping operators control tokens and alternatives.

tr.td: match the same text as a previously captured token

description.p: Anchors and assertions match positions rather than characters.

tr.td: beginning and end of the input text, or of a line with 'lineanchors'
beginning and end of a word
word boundary or not a word boundary

description.p: Use 'ignorecase' for case-insensitive matching, 'dotexceptnewline' when . must not match newline characters, 'lineanchors' when ^ and $ should apply to individual lines, and 'forceCellOutput' to force scalar text results into cells.

example_item.example_item_description: Find start indices and matched text.
example_item.example_item_data: regexp('bat cat coat', 'c[aeiou]+t') regexp('She sells sea shells.', '[Ss]h.', 'match') regexp('01/11/2000', '(?<month>\d+)/(?<day>\d+)/(?<year>\d+)', 'names')

example_item.example_item_description: Match several related speed units with one expression.
Extract email addresses from a cell array of text.
Use tokens, named tokens, and split output.
example_item.example_item_data: regexp('3.14', '(\d+)\.(\d+)', 'tokens', 'once') regexp('color: blue', '(?<key>\w+):\s*(?<value>\w+)', 'names') regexp('one, two; three', '[,;]\s*', 'split')
